coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional N doped carbon dots for cellular sensing, light-emitting-diode, and additive manufacturing
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Yawen
Zhang, Shuai
Li, Shanggeng
Wei, Lai
Zhang, Lin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have gained tremendous attention over a wide range from fluorescence sensors to optoelectronic devices. Herein, N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) of 2.3 nm were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method through Tryptamine and Citric Acid. The N-CDs were highly selective to Fe3+ because of electron transfer. On the other hand, it could be applied for Fe3+ bioimaging in Hela cells due to good biocompatibility. In addition, a blue light-emitting-diode (LED) was fabricated by mixing siloxane with N-CDs. Furthermore, a shiny-three-dimensional grid structure was successfully prepared by direct writing technology. This study provides a novel idea for the potential applications of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Nanoparticles
Fe3+ sensing
Light-emitting-diode
Additive manufacturing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM photograph, (b) the diameter distribution histogram, (c) XPS full survey spectrum of N-CDs, high-resolution XPS spectra (d) C 1s, (e) N 1s, (f) O 1s.
Confocal microscopy pictures of Hela cells. Cells after incubated (c, d) with and (a, b) without N-CDs. (e, f) Cells cultivated with N-CDs, subsequently co-incubated with 100 μM FeCl3.

(a) photoluminescence, (b) fluorescence emission spectrum, (c) color coordinate of the LED, (d) photograph under sunlight, (e) image under 365 nm ultraviolet light, (f) three months later of the printed grid structure.
The diagrammatic plan for the synthesis and application of N-CDs.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional N doped carbon dots for cellular sensing, light-emitting-diode, and additive manufacturing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center of Laser Fusion, China Academy of Engineering Physics, Mianyang 621900, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center of Laser Fusion, China Academy of Engineering Physics, Mianyang 621900, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Nuclear Science and Technology, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Nuclear Science and Technology
University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Nuclear Science and Technology, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have gained tremendous attention over a wide range from fluorescence sensors to optoelectronic devices. Herein, N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) of 2.3 nm were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal method through Tryptamine and Citric Acid. The N-CDs were highly selective to Fe3+ because of electron transfer. On the other hand, it could be applied for Fe3+ bioimaging in Hela cells due to good biocompatibility. In addition, a blue light-emitting-diode (LED) was fabricated by mixing siloxane with N-CDs. Furthermore, a shiny-three-dimensional grid structure was successfully prepared by direct writing technology. This study provides a novel idea for the potential applications of CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a rising star of carbon-based zero-dimensional material, have gained widespread attention owing to low production cost, easy functionalization, and good biocompatibility [1,2]. Because of these fascinating merits, they have been widely applied in sensing [3], bioimaging [4], optoelectronic devices [5], and other fields [6].

In the past few years, many researchers have focused on the synthesis of CDs, such as electrochemical approach [7], laser ablation [8], acidic oxidation [9], hydrothermal methods [10], solvothermal methods [11], and so on. Among the above, solvothermal and hydrothermal methods are economical and environmentally friendly synthesis means [12]. In addition, a series of hydrophilic functional groups (such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, or amidogen) can be formed in the synthesis process, which is favorable for further application. Generally speaking, the solvent of the hydrothermal is water, while the solvent of solvothermal method is mostly organic reagent, such as organic amine, alcohol, or benzene. Therefore, hydrothermal method is minor harmful to body and environment in the process of synthesis and application; and keeps in accord with the concept of building an environmentally friendly society.

In this work, N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with an absolute quantum yield of 12.13 % were prepared via a hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ) using Tryptamine and Citric Acid as precursors. Thanks to the surface of N-CDs being abundant in oxygen and nitrogen functional groups, we developed a label-free strategy for sensing and bioimaging Fe3+. Furthermore, a blue light-emitting-diode (LED) of good color rendering performance was fabricated when mixed with siloxane and N-CDs. In the end, we introduced ink direct writing technology to build a shiny 3D-printing complex. This work is expected to advance the knowledge of sensing, bioimaging, LED applications, and additive manufacturing.
N-CDs were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method. (Experimental details, characterization methods are supplied in Supporting Information).

Fig. 1 a depicts that the morphology of the synthesized N-CDs is uniformly distributed and spherical. Meanwhile, the N-CDs exhibit a relatively narrow size distribution between 1 and 4 nm with an average diameter of 2.3 nm (Fig. 1b). The full scan spectra in Fig. 1 c distinctly present three typical peaks located at 284.8, 399.9, and 532.7 eV, which are associated with C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, severally. The C 1s spectrum has three characteristic peaks due to carbonyl groups (CO, 289.3 eV), sp3 carbon (CN/CO, 286.8 eV), and sp2 carbon (CC, 284.8 eV), respectively [13]. The N 1s spectrum can be decomposed into two characteristic peaks at 399.6, 401.8 eV, corresponding to pyrrolic N and graphitic N [14]. In Fig. 2 d, the O 1s spectrum presents two peaks at 532.2 and 533.3 eV, demonstrating the CO and COC/C–OH [15]. The XPS results imply abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface of the N-CDs, which is consistent with the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S2). Furthermore, Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S3) was used to study the hybrid morphology of carbon.

From Fig. S4, the UV–vis spectrum has a typical absorption at 254 nm, ascribed to the π-π* transition in the aromatic conjugated system [16]. The fluorescence spectra exhibit the maximum emission wavelength (455 nm) upon excitation at 310 nm. Besides, the CDs exhibit excitation independent property when the excitation wavelength change from 270 to 410 nm (Fig. S4). Furthermore, the N-CDs are stable in a high ion strength (1 M NaCl), and a wide pH range of 3.0–11.0 (Fig. S5.). In addition, they are more resistant to photo-bleaching (continuous irradiation 80 min) than traditional fluorescent dyes [12]. Also, they have good oxidation resistance (0.3 M H2O2) than previously reported CDs [7].

As noted above, there are nitrogen and oxygen functional groups on the surface of N-CDs, which can form complexes with some metal cations through coordination. For another, Fe3+ is a suitable electron acceptor because it has a half-field 3d shell [17]. Consequently, the particular response of N-CDs to Fe3+ was further investigated. The fluorescence emission is quenched gradually along with the concentration of Fe3+ increased without the position of characteristic peak changes. The changes in the fluorescence emission intensity of N-CDs at 455 nm are proportional to the content of Fe3+ from 500 nM to 200 μM with the detection limit of 0.25 μM (Fig. S6a). Meanwhile, Fe3+ induced fluorescence intensity quenching of the N-CDs is highly specific (Fig. S6b).

The N-CDs with good biocompatibility are suitable for bioimaging (Fig. S7). As displayed in Fig. 2d, intense fluorescence from intracellular can be clearly observed. When we added extra Fe3+ to the cells preprocessed by N-CDs, the microscope image showed weak fluorescence in the fields (Fig. 2f). Negative controls, made by the cells without any treatment under the same conditions, show no fluorescence emission (Fig. 2b). Additionally, the cytotoxicity of N-CDs is negligible, which can be confirmed by the results that the cells still maintain normal morphology in Fig. 2(c, e).

The fluorescence lifetime was used to study the quenching mechanism. As shown in Table S1, the decay curves fit a two-exponential decay model. In the existence of 100 μM Fe3+, the lifetime about 16 ns is scarcely changed, manifesting that the quenching of N-CDs is not fluorescence resonance energy transfer [18]. The speculated sensing mechanism is displayed in Fig. S9. In brief, when Fe3+ was added to N-CDs solution, oxygen and nitrogen on the surface of N-CDs complexed with it, resulting in the splitting of d orbital of Fe3+, restricting the electron transition of the excited states in the N-CDs. Then, the excited electrons in N-CDs transferred to the d orbital of Fe3+, leading to fluorescence quenching.

Considering the particular optical properties and excellent stability of N-CDs, we mixed N-CDs with siloxane as LED chip coatings. As shown in Fig. 3 (a–c), the constructed chip emits bright blue fluorescence; at the same time, the fluorescence spectral position showed no significant deviation compared with the solution. For another, the color coordinate of this device is (0.22, 0.26) and the Color Rendering Index is 78.5. Compared with traditional phosphors made from rare earth elements, this device based on N-CDs can shorten the preparation process and reduce production costs.

Direct writing is an important additive manufacturing technology, which can be used to form fine three-dimensional complex structures [19]. We mixed siloxane with N-CDs, accompanied by acrylate as photocurable reagents, the UV light was introduced in the direct writing process, and a three-dimensional cellular structure was constructed. As displayed in Fig. 3d, the cellular structure is almost white under sunlight, emitting bright cyan light under a portable 365 UV lamp. The tactic benefits struct flexible optical device design and advanced anti-counterfeiting in additive manufacturing.

In summary, Tryptamine and Citric Acid were used to synthesize fluorescent N-CDs through a facile hydrothermal method in this study. The obtained N-CDs can be applied for the sensing and bioimaging of Fe3+. On the other hand, the application of the prepared N-CDs in LED coating and additive manufacturing was also presented. Thus, this study showed that N-CDs would pave a new path to exploring potential applications.

Yawen Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Shuai Zhang: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Shanggeng Li: Methodology, Data curation. Lai Wei: Methodology, Data curation. Lin Zhang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51803198) for financial support and eceshi (www.eceshi.com) for the TEM measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2022.132838.
